claim_chain: It is uncommon to uncover new symptoms for any condition, especially for afflictions that have been known about for centuries
We identified 10 new prodromal symptoms strongly associated with eclampsia, with odds ratios over 10
These new symptoms were more strongly associated with eclampsia than headache, visual changes or epigastric pain
Incorporating these 10 symptoms as part of history taking may be useful as part of clinical management
The symptoms we have identified may be useful to direct who to give magnesium sulphate
Eclampsia was uncommon in the absence of any prodromal symptoms
These symptoms may be useful in deciding whether magnesium sulphate is required or could be safely withheld

typical_pattern: Opening statement of novelty or significance
Statement of main findings with quantitative support
Comparison to existing knowledge or practice

likely_priorities: Recall bias and retrospective symptom assessment
Differential timing of symptom recall between groups
Generalizability beyond tertiary centers to peripheral and community settings
Validation in other populations and settings
Interventional bias from magnesium sulphate administration based on classic symptoms
likely_attacks: Asking about prodromal symptoms after the seizure happened rather than prospectively
Recall periods differed between groups introducing differential misclassification
Recruited from three tertiary centres limiting applicability across care settings
Clinicians may have commenced magnesium sulphate for classic symptoms but not novel ones
Those affected by eclampsia might have recalled symptoms differently due to significant neurological episode
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly lists multiple limitations: asking about symptoms after seizure, potential recall bias, differential recall periods between groups, recruitment from tertiary centers only, and potential interventional bias. The authors state 'Our study had several limitations' and enumerate them. They also note 'Ideally, the associations between symptoms and eclampsia are validated in other populations and in different settings' and 'it is critical that applicability across care settings is assessed, specifically in peripheral and community health services.' These self-identified weaknesses form the basis for inferring likely reviewer concerns.

examples.quoted_phrase: may be useful as part of history taking
we identified 10 new prodromal symptoms strongly associated with eclampsia
Their presence may mean there is an elevated risk of eclampsia
it may be reasonable to withhold magnesium sulphate
Eclampsia is uncommon without any prodromal symptoms

phrase_evidence.phrase: It is uncommon to uncover new symptoms for any condition, especially for afflictions that have been known about for centuries
we identified 10 new prodromal symptoms strongly associated with eclampsia
with odds ratios over 10 when compared to women with preeclampsia
These new symptoms were more strongly associated with eclampsia than headache, visual changes or epigastric pain
Incorporating these 10 symptoms as part of history taking may be useful
Their presence may suggest preeclampsia with neurological involvement
Their presence may mean there is an elevated risk of eclampsia
While this was not intended to be a diagnostic prediction study
the symptoms we have identified may be useful to direct who to give magnesium sulphate
those with preeclampsia but who are asymptomatic appear to be at very low risk
Currently, there is no consensus which pregnancies complicated by preeclampsia should be offered magnesium sulphate
Without reliable clinical indicators to predict eclampsia
Classic prodromal symptoms of headache, visual disturbances, and epigastric pain were only modestly associated with eclampsia
The absence of any prodromal symptoms may mean the eclampsia risk is very low
it may be reasonable to withhold magnesium sulphate
Such a selective approach to offering magnesium sulphate based on the presence of symptoms may reduce numbers given the drug
Our study had several limitations
we asked about prodromal symptoms after the seizure happened
a prospective screening study to find new symptoms would have been highly challenging
This may have introduced interventional bias
Another potential limitation is the risk of recall bias
However, the relative differences in odds ratios between symptoms within groups may still be valid
This difference may introduce differential misclassification
Ideally, the associations between symptoms and eclampsia are validated in other populations and in different settings
it is critical that applicability across care settings is assessed
Strengths of our study were that we recruited participants within seven days
We were agnostic as to which novel symptom could be associated with eclampsia, which minimised bias
suggesting the pathophysiology of eclampsia is diffuse
This hints at a biological gradient
these symptoms may be useful in deciding whether magnesium sulphate is required or could be safely withheld

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. All numeric values appear in the input. No external references, author names, or invented data were included. The profile is based solely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
